statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
This is the hard version of the problem. The difference between the versions is that in this version, $ n \le 5 \cdot 10^{5}, m \le 5 \cdot 10^{4} $ . You can hack only if you solved all versions of this problem.

There are two strings $ s $ and $ t $ , both consisting of lowercase Latin letters. You also have an empty string $ p $ .

You can perform the following operation, which consists of several stages:

- choose any two integers $ l $ and $ r $ ( $ 1 \le l \le r \le |s| $ );
- copy the substring $ s_{l},\ldots, s_{r} $ and append it to the end of string $ p $ ;
- among the last $ r - l + 1 $ characters of string $ p $ , change at most one character to any lowercase Latin letter.

For example, if the string $ s = $ "dhhtyhwbsl" and $ p = $ "", you can choose $ l = 3, r = 6 $ and add "htyh" to the end of $ p $ , and then change the character "y" to "a", resulting in $ p = $ "htah".

Your task is to determine the minimum number of operations required for string $ p $ to become equal to $ t $ .

Input Format
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^4 $ ). The description of the test cases follows.

The first line of each test case contains two integers $ n $ and $ m $ ( $ 1 \le n \le 5 \cdot 10^{5}, 1 \le m \le 5 \cdot 10^{4} $ ) — the lengths of strings $ s $ and $ t $ .

The second line of each test case contains the string $ s $ of length $ n $ , consisting of lowercase Latin letters.

The third line of each test case contains the string $ t $ of length $ m $ , consisting of lowercase Latin letters.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $ n $ across all test cases does not exceed $ 5 \cdot 10^{5} $ , and the sum of $ m $ across all test cases does not exceed $ 5 \cdot 10^{4} $ .

Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer — the answer to the problem.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
4
1 1
a
b
5 5
aaaaa
abzba
10 13
dhhtyhwbsl
htahbsehtyhzb
7 2
contest
on
Output
1
3
3
1

Note
In the first test case, you can take the substring $ «\mathtt{a}» $ from the string $ s $ , change a single letter in this string to $ «\mathtt{b}» $ , and append it to the end of the string $ p $ , resulting in $ p $ becoming equal to $ t $ .

In the second test case, $ t $ can be obtained in $ 3 $ operations as follows:

$$$
«\mathtt{a\color{red}{b}}» + «\mathtt{\color{red}{z}}» + «\mathtt{\color{red}{b}a}»
$$$

The modified characters are highlighted in red.

In the third test case, the string $ t $ can be obtained as follows:

$$$
«\mathtt{ht\color{red}{a}h}» + «\mathtt{bs\color{red}{e}}» + «\mathtt{htyh\color{red}{z}b}»
$$$

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2196E2 - Fuzzy Concatenation (Hard version)
The following greedy algorithm works: we will find the longest prefix of the string $$$t$$$ such that this prefix is contained in $$$s$$$ as a substring (possibly with one character replaced) and then remove this prefix from $$$t$$$ and repeat until $$$t$$$ is empty. The number of such "pieces" will be the answer. This greediness is correct because if we take a prefix of shorter length, the next piece can only decrease in size, not increase.
To efficiently find the longest prefix, we will use the following algorithm:
We will start from the beginning of the prefix and try to change the current character to some character from the alphabet, and then attempt to find the LCP of the string $$$t$$$ with the modified character and some suffix of the string $$$s$$$. Let $$$f(t', pos)$$$ denote the time to find such an LCP if a character in the string is changed at position $$$pos$$$ to some character from the alphabet; then the time complexity of the entire algorithm can be estimated as $$$O(|t| \cdot \sum_{i = 1}^{|t|} f(t', i))$$$.
We will use a suffix array. We will construct it for the string $$$s + "\#" + t$$$, and also compute the LCP for this array. Additionally, we will compute the reverse array $$$sa\_rev$$$: that is, such that $$$sa\_rev[i]$$$ contains the position of the suffix of length $$$i$$$ in the suffix array $$$sa$$$.
Let $$$t_{suff}$$$ be the suffix of the original $$$t$$$, where we need to search for the maximum "piece". Also, let $$$t_{suff, ps}$$$ be the suffix of the original $$$t$$$, in which one character was changed at position $$$ps$$$.
In the suffix array, there are two classes of suffixes: for $$$s$$$ and for $$$t$$$. We will separate the positions of all suffixes for $$$s$$$ into a separate array. Now let's search for our maximum "piece" in $$$t_{suff, ps}$$$. We will perform a binary search on the suffix array to determine where the position of the string $$$t_{suff, ps}$$$ would be in it if we wanted to insert $$$t_{suff, ps}$$$ into the suffix array constructed solely from $$$s$$$.
Next, we need to understand how the check for this binary search will look. Suppose we want to check during the binary search whether the position after $$$sa_i$$$ is the position we need; let $$$sa_{j}$$$ and $$$sa_{k}$$$ be the positions of the nearest suffixes to the left and right that belong to $$$s$$$. We need to check that $$$sa_{j} \le t_{suff, ps} \le sa_{k}$$$. It seems that comparing such strings cannot be done faster than $$$\log$$$, but since we do not have arbitrary strings, we can do the following:
We will find the LCP of $$$sa_{j}$$$ and $$$t_{suff}$$$ (we can do this because $$$sa$$$ is constructed from all suffixes of $$$s$$$ and $$$t$$$, and we can find the LCP of any two arbitrary suffixes in the suffix array in $$$O(1)$$$). If this LCP is greater than $$$ps$$$ for the current $$$t_{suff, ps}$$$, then we know the relationship between the strings $$$sa_{j}$$$ and $$$t_{suff, ps}$$$ (greater, less, or equal). If the LCP is less, we will check that the corresponding character of $$$sa_{j}$$$ at position $$$ps$$$ is equal to the corresponding character in $$$t_{suff, ps}$$$ (if it is not equal, we again know the relationship between the strings).
If it turns out to be equal, we need to compare the strings $$$sa_{sa\_rev_{sa_{j} + ps}}$$$ and $$$t_{suff + ps}$$$. This can also be done in $$$O(1)$$$ by looking at their positions in the suffix array $$$sa$$$.
After we have found the required position in the suffix using binary search, we can similarly calculate the maximum LCP of $$$t_{suff, ps}$$$ and the substring of $$$s$$$ also in $$$O(1)$$$.
Thus, $$$f(t', pos)$$$ can be estimated as $$$O(\log (|s| + |t|) \cdot k)$$$, where $$$k$$$ is the size of the alphabet.
Therefore, the problem can be solved in $$$O(|t| \cdot \log (|s| + |t|) \cdot k)$$$
